#T_SP=custom.Span|label
#Text=FOOD FOR THE SICK.
#Text=THE feeding of persons in health is of great importance, but when one succumbs to disease, then feeding becomes a question of supreme moment.
#Text=The appetite in health is usually a safe guide to follow, but is so perverted by disease conditions that it is unwise to consider its cravings.
#Text=If these cravings are indulged, the food longed for is almost always a disappointment, as all things taste about the same, until the time of convalescence.
#Text=Never consult a patient as to his menu, nor enter into a conversation relating to his diet, within his hearing.
#Text=The physician in attendance studies the symptoms so closely that he is able to determine what is required to meet the needs of the case.
#Text=He orders nourishment given regularly, usually in small quantities, at frequent intervals.
#Text=Appreciating the value of sleep, he never allows his patient to be awakened for feeding, unless the exigencies of the case create this demand.
#Text=In acute cases of disease, food plays a very important part towards recovery.
#Text=The quantity and kind taken must vary greatly, according to the nature of the disease.

#Text=Sometimes it proves expedient in cases of diarrhœa or dysentery to have the patient abstain for days from all food, except a very thin, starchy gruel, the object being to starve the germ which causes the disease; then, again, a patient, after a surgical operation, where there is a great loss of blood, needs a large supply of food.

#Text=Where the temperature is high, metabolism goes on so rapidly there is always a demand for a large quantity of During the gradual return to a normal condition, through the long tedious hours of convalescence, the patient devotes much thought to when and what he shall be allowed to eat, and it is at this time that the taste is gratified as far as is advisable.
#Text=The best means of stimulating the appetite is to have good food, well cooked, and attractively served.
#Text=The  service should be the best at the command of the nurse, and too much attention cannot be given to every detail.
#Text=The trays should be of correct size, so when laid not to have the appearance of being overcrowded; on the other hand, if a small amount is to be served, have a small tray.
#Text=The tray cloth should be spotless, and just fit over edge of tray.
#Text=If the correct size is not at hand, one should be folded to fit tray, or a napkin may often be utilized for this purpose.
#Text=Select the choicest china, silver, and glassware, making changes as often as possible.
#Text=It often proves pleasing to carry out a color scheme.
#Text=Nervous patients are apt to be depressed in the early morning, therefore for this reason make the breakfast tray as attractive as possible by using bright flowers.
#Text=In setting a tray after laying the tray cloth, locate the plate.
#Text=Place the knife at the right of plate, sharp edge toward plate.
#Text=Place the spoon at the right of knife, bowl up.
#Text=Place the fork at the left of plate, tines up.
#Text=A bread and butter plate or individual butter is placed over fork a little to the left.
#Text=The napkin is always placed at left of fork; then cup and saucer at right of spoon, with cup so placed that it may be easily raised by handle.
#Text=The water glass is placed over knife at little to right.
#Text=Arrange the other dishes to suit the convenience of the patient.
#Text=All eating is very much influenced by the taste.
#Text=Some foods, easy of digestion, if repugnant to a patient, prove indigestible.
#Text=The temperature of food has a marked influence upon digestion.
#Text=As a rule, hot foods should be served hot, cold foods served cold; but this often must be varied according to the case.
#Text=Under certain conditions very cold or very hot food might retard digestion, thus increasing the amount of energy necessary for absorption.

#Text=Coarse foods, like Graham bread, some cereals, and vegetables containing much cellulose, pass through the alimentary canal so quickly that much of their nutritive value is lost, as so large a portion escapes absorption.
#Text=Sugar is completely absorbed by the system, while starch holds second place.
#Text=The proteid of meat and eggs is well absorbed, only three per cent being lost; in milk the loss is eight per cent.

#Text=Fat, when taken in the form of butter, is almost completely absorbed, while in the absorption of fat in the form of milk, eggs, and cheese, there is a loss of six per cent; in the fat of meat, a loss of seventeen per cent.
#Text=Bacon furnishes an exception to this rule, as it ranks next to the fat of butter and cream.
#Text=The nurse should be a student of the classification of foods, their fuel value and digestibility, thus being able to determine and regulate the needed rations for her patients.
#Text=In feeding the sick, strict economy should be considered only when necessary.
#Text=That which the patient really needs should be furnished always, if possible.
#Text=Even in homes where the income is limited, there is a general self-denial for the one who is ill.
#Text=It is too-frequent error to over-indulge a patient, for it weakens rather than strengthens.

#Text=From this fact, together with lack of knowledge and appliance, those who are treated and cared for in a fine hospital are very apt to recover more quickly than those treated and cared for at home.

#Text=In hospitals, where large numbers are to be fed, many of whom are not able to contribute towards the support of the institution and still others who cannot pay their proportionate part, strict economy in food supplies becomes imperative.
#Text=Many cheap foods are equally nutritious to the more expensive ones, and if well cooked and served are gratifying to all the senses to which one wishes to appeal.
#Text=In hospitals the dietaries are classified by the doctors, to assist the nurses in caring for their patients, as house, soft solid, soft diet, and liquid.  1.
#Text=House, including:--soups, meat, fish, eggs, cereals, vegetables, fruit, desserts, etc.  2.

#Text=Soft solid, including:--creamed sweetbreads, eggs, creamed toast, asparagus, baked custards, etc  Milk, as soon as it comes from the animal, should be put in sterile vessels, cooled as quickly as possible, covered, and kept at a low temperature.
#Text=In the hands of the consumer it always contains a large number of micro-organisms, the greater number of which tend to increase lactic fermentation, which causes souring.
#Text=If milk is kept under favorable conditions and for not too long a time, these do not sufficiently multiply to cause anxiety.
#Text=The pathogenic germs in milk are often causes of typhoid fever, diphtheria, scarlet fever, tuberculosis, and cholera.
#Text=They are killed at 167° F., a much lower temperature than the micro-organisms which produce souring, and they also occur in smaller numbers.
#Text=When in doubt of the milk supply, it is always best to scald it.
#Text=If milk is allowed to stand for a few hours, the fat rises to the surface in the form of cream; this is due to its lower specific gravity.
#Text=Such cream is called gravity cream.
#Text=When a separator is used for removing cream, the cream thus obtained is called centrifugal cream.

#Text=It must not be inferred from this that cream contains nothing but fat, for it holds relatively as large a proportion of sugar and proteid as milk itself, the deficiency being in the proportion of water.
#Text=Cream is an expensive form of fat, and must be regarded as a luxury.
#Text=Lactose (sugar of milk) is equal in nutritive value to cane sugar.
#Text=It has some advantages over cane sugar, for under ordinary conditions it does not ferment, it is not so quickly absorbed, and it is a diuretic.
#Text=Lactose is not so sweet to the taste as cane sugar, which renders it of value in the sick-room; it is, however, an expensive fuel food.
#Text=The proteid of milk is the cheapest form in which animal proteid may be obtained.
#Text=It consists principally of casein, albumen to a smaller extent, some globulin, and traces of peptone.
#Text=Potassium, sodium, calcium, magnesium, iron, sulphur, phosphorus, and chlorine are all found in the ash of milk in the form of oxides, chlorides, or acids.
#Text=Skim Milk.
#Text=Milk from which most of the fat has been removed is known as skim milk.
#Text=The quantity of fat removed depends on the manner in which the work is accomplished.
#Text=Separator skimmed milk has less fat than that from deep cold setting.
#Text=Skim milk has more proteid than whole milk, and a slightly larger proportion of milk sugar.
#Text=Skim milk may be advantageously used by the poorer classes.
#Text=Its deficiency in fat may be made up, easily, by obtaining it from cheaper sources than whole milk.
#Text=Buttermilk.
#Text=Buttermilk is obtained from cream during its manufacture into butter.
#Text=Its composition differs but little from that of skim milk.
#Text=It contains less proteid and sugar and more fat.
#Text=It has a slightly acid taste, which makes it an agreeable beverage, and it is well borne by people of weak digestion.
#Text=It acts as a laxative.
#Text=Whey.
#Text=When milk is clotted by the action of rennet, wine, or an acid, and after standing a few minutes is strained through a double thickness of cheese cloth, the liquid obtained is known as whey.
#Text=It is a slightly laxative fluid of small nutritive value.
#Text=Koumiss, Kefir, and Matzoon.
#Text=Meat extractives are the greatest known stimulants to gastric juice.

#Text=Beef extract, being concentrated, may be retained often, if taken in small quantities at frequent intervals, where beef tea could not be borne; on the other hand, beef tea may be taken in larger quantities with satisfying effects, where beef extract would prove insufficient.
#Text=Many preparations made from beef are on the market in the form of liquids, powders, meals, or pastes.

#Text=To some of these a considerable amount of fat is added, which increases their nutritive value; still others are useful only for the flavor and color they impart, and would find better place in the kitchen to be used in the making of soups and sauces than in the sick-room.
#Text=Home-made beef tea, if carefully prepared, is usually liked better by a patient, costs much less, and, as a rule, is more nutritious than the manufactured article.
#Text=It will keep without decomposition, however, but a short time.
#Text=Physicians frequently order the preparations on the market, to give variety, and to try, if possible, to please  the patient.
#Text=Again, if home-made beef tea is ordered he is in doubt as to the way in which it will be made, while the manufactured product is uniformly constant.
#Text=It is never safe to resort to beef tea as the principal article of diet for more than a few days, as it would mean slow starvation to the patient.
#Text=Milk, egg, cracker, or bread is added, frequently, to beef extract or beef tea to increase their food value.
#Text=Beef, for the making of beef extract and beef tea, should be cut from the upper or lower part of the round of a heavy corn-fed steer.
#Text=This insures good flavor and a large quantity of juice.
#Text=One-half pound of such beef will yield two ounces (four tablespoons) of juice, making the price about five cents per ounce.
#Text=The juice from the lower part of the round is quite as satisfactory and less expensive than that from the upper part.
#Text=Beef extract may be served in a colored glass or small china cup.
#Text=In this way the color, which is objectionable to many, may be concealed.

#Text=THE so-called vegetables, with the exception of peas, beans, and lentils, contain a small amount of nutriment, but are valuable, nevertheless, for the large amount of water and the mineral matter they contain.
#Text=Vegetables are necessary for the body's needs, as they give bulk to the food and possess especial antiscorbutic properties.
#Text=In selecting summer vegetables, choose those that are fresh and crisp, and when possible purchase from the producer, as they should be cooked as soon after gathering as possible.
#Text=Beans and peas, when fresh, young, and tender, may be used to advantage in the sick-room.
#Text=If served when old, they should be pressed through a strainer after being   cooked, and made into a purée or soup.
#Text=In this way the cellulose may be discarded.
#Text=Asparagus appears in the market as one of the early native vegetables, and may be introduced into the dietary of the convalescent.
#Text=In the spring of the year many patients long for dandelions.
#Text=Their peculiar bitter flavor acts as a stimulant to a flagging appetite.
#Text=A desire is often expressed for beet greens, which may be indulged.
#Text=Cauliflower may be used by the convalescent, while cabbage, which belongs to the same family, should be avoided except by the people who serve it frequently when in health.
#Text=During the cooking of cabbage certain ferments are developed which cause the vegetable when eaten to give rise to stomach eructations.
#Text=Onions are wholesome and quite nutritious to persons with whom they agree.
#Text=The Bermuda and Spanish onions are much more delicate in flavor than the common garden onion.
#Text=Onions impart a strong odor to the breath, due to volatile substances absorbed by the blood, and by the blood carried to the lungs, where they are set free.
#Text=The use of broiled or stewed tomatoes in many diseases is not objectionable; however, in cases where citric acid is to be avoided, tomatoes must be excluded from the dietary.
#Text=A reliable brand of canned tomatoes is quite as satisfactory as the fresh vegetable if the canned goods are thoroughly re-oxygenated.
#Text=Spinach is found in the market throughout the year, but is especially desirable during the winter, when most of the green vegetables are expensive and of inferior quality.
#Text=Among the summer vegetables, green corn and cucumbers should be avoided, as they are the source of much stomach and bowel trouble.
#Text=Unless one has access to the large city markets, the supply of vegetables during the winter months is limited to squash, turnips, carrots, parsnips, beets, cabbage, and onions.

#Text=Squash is the only one among them all that   would not be subject to unfavorable criticism if used in the feeding of the sick, and even this vegetable must be excluded from the dietary of the diabetic.
#Text=Lettuce is classed among salad greens.
#Text=It may be obtained usually throughout the year, as it is raised during the winter in hothouses, at which time it is especially palatable.
#Text=It should be fresh and crisp.
#Text=The outer leaves, which are usually somewhat wilted, should be discarded.
#Text=The food value of lettuce is much increased when served with a French Dressing.
#Text=Many a thin person would find a real fattening agent in olive oil.
#Text=The young, tender stalks of celery often prove an appetizer, while radishes would better be avoided on account of the large amount of cellulose they contain.
#Text=Among other salad greens may be named chickory, water-cress, romaine, and escarole, all of which may be occasionally introduced into the menu of the convalescent.
#Text=Cooking of.
#Text=Vegetables, like all starchy foods, should be cooked in freshly boiling water, salt often being added to give flavor.
#Text=By the application of hot water the starch grains swell and burst, which gives the starch an opportunity to escape through the layers of cellulose.
#Text=From this statement it may be seen that cookery plays a very important part towards the complete digestion of starchy foods.
#Text=Vegetables contain such a small quantity of proteid that the loss during cooking should be made as slight as possible.
#Text=Like animal proteid, it is coagulated by heat.
#Text=It is more soluble in cold water than animal proteid.
#Text=Thus if vegetables are allowed to soak in cold water for too long a time, there will be an appreciable loss in nutritive value, mineral matter as well as proteid.
